results.title: LingoPlus Expands Nationwide: Certified Translation Services Now Available Across Germany - markets.businessinsider.com
results.raw_content:
![](data:image/svg+xml;base64,)

# LingoPlus Expands Nationwide: Certified Translation Services Now Available Across Germany

Paderborn, NRW, July 18, 2026 (GLOBE NEWSWIRE) -- What began as a small translation initiative in 2010 has developed into a nationwide provider of certified translations. [LingoPlus](https://lingoplus.de) delivers professional translation services for businesses and individuals across Germany, with a strong focus on certified translations required for official, legal, and institutional use.

The company was founded by Basel Layous, a graduate in Computer Science and Economics who has worked as a sworn translator and authorized interpreter since 2010. In addition to his work in the language services sector, he has founded several ventures, including the SaaS company Magnet Domain, bringing digital expertise and entrepreneurial experience to the translation industry.

“Certified translations must be precise, reliable, and accessible,” says Layous. “Our goal is to combine the accuracy of sworn translators with efficient digital processes to serve clients throughout Germany. By bridging the gap between traditional legal requirements and modern digital workflows, LingoPlus ensures that clients spend less time on administration and more time on their core business or personal objectives.”

Today, LingoPlus provides certified translations and specialized language services including:

To ensure consistent quality and efficient turnaround times, LingoPlus combines a dedicated in-house team of sworn translators with a carefully selected network of authorized interpreters and specialized linguists. This hybrid structure provides the reliability of in-house quality control while maintaining the agility to handle specialized projects—from the urgent translation of an individual certificate to large-scale, multi-document business rollouts.

The expansion reflects a growing demand for digital-first translation services. As international mobility and cross-border business continue to increase, the reliance on high-quality, verified translations has become a standard necessity. By investing in secure data transmission and automated project tracking, LingoPlus has streamlined the ordering and delivery process, allowing for transparent tracking of projects from initiation to final post-delivery.

By combining linguistic expertise with streamlined digital workflows, LingoPlus continues to expand its online translation services, making certified translations more accessible to clients across Germany.

**About LingoPlus:**  LingoPlus is a certified translation agency in Germany providing professional translation services nationwide. The company specializes in certified translations, legal translations, and business translations for companies and individuals. By combining experienced linguists with modern digital workflows, the agency delivers accurate and reliable translations for official, legal, and business purposes.

**Press Inquiries**

Basel Layous  
 info [at] lingoplus.de  
<https://lingoplus.de>

results.raw_content:
[![How-To Geek logo](https://static0.howtogeekimages.com/assets/images/htg-logo-full-colored-light.svg?v=3.6 "How-To Geek")](/)

Sign in now

# Stop paying for search APIs—this self-hosted tool lets my local LLM search the web for free

![A GEEKOM A5 mini PC being held in a person's hand.](https://static0.howtogeekimages.com/wordpress/wp-content/uploads/wm/2026/01/a7305463.jpg?&fit=crop&w=1600&h=900) Credit: Patrick Campanale / How-To Geek

[![4](https://static0.howtogeekimages.com/wordpress%2Fwp-content%2Fauthors%2F67160a0c90bf3-Image%2019-07-2024%20at%2014.08%202.jpg?fit=crop&w=90&h=90)](/author/adam-davidson/)

By  [Adam Davidson](/author/adam-davidson/)

Adam has a degree in Engineering and has a life-long interest in technology. He has been writing for over a decade for various print and online publications, with a focus on consumer tech. He joined How-To Geek in 2024 while working at [Pocket-lint](https://www.pocket-lint.com/author/adam-davidson/).  
 As well as being a long-term fan of Apple products, he also has a strong interest in smart home tech, running a Home Assistant server at home to automate all his smart home devices. He believes that the ideal smart home should work with minimal interaction from the user, with automations running as if by magic rather than requiring you to push buttons on a control panel. You can find more of his work on [Muck Rack](https://muckrack.com/adam-davidson-40/articles).

Sign in to your How-To Geek account

I use local LLMs for a lot of things. While the models I can run on my hardware are nowhere near as powerful as ChatGPT, Gemini, or Claude, a local LLM can still do a good job, and most importantly, my data doesn't end up on third-party servers. One thing my local LLM can't do by default is search the web, but a self-hosted tool fixes this for free.

## SearXNG is a metasearch engine

### It won't answer your prompts on its own

![Two hands holding phones, one with Bing and the other with Google displayed on the screen.](https://static0.howtogeekimages.com/wordpress/wp-content/uploads/2024/03/shutterstock_2295771191.jpg?q=49&fit=crop&w=825&dpr=2) Credit: gguy / Shutterstock.com

SearXNG is a free and open-source, [self-hosted internet metasearch engine](https://www.howtogeek.com/this-self-hosted-search-engine-helped-me-escape-googles-ai-redesign/). Unlike standard search engines that crawl the web and index pages to generate search results, SearXNG takes your request and can aggregate results from multiple search engines and databases, such as Google, Bing, DuckDuckGo, Wikipedia, Reddit, and more.

SearXNG helps protect your privacy by acting as an intermediary between you and the underlying search engines, without tracking or profiling you. It uses multiple search engines in parallel, so the results can be fairly fast.

What SearXNG doesn't do is any thinking of its own; it merely finds useful results and merges them into a collection of titles, snippets, and links. You can't use SearXNG on its own to answer a question such as "Which actors appear in both Star Wars and [The Lord of the Rings](https://www.howtogeek.com/the-lord-of-the-rings-best-6-ways-television-show-tribute-trilogy/)?" There are [options like Perplexica](https://github.com/BunsDev/perplexica-search-engine-ai) that can do this, but they're too heavy for my modest mini PC.

## Setting it up took a single command

### You may need to make a small tweak

Setting up SearXNG was easy to do. I [run my local models in Ollama](https://www.howtogeek.com/install-and-use-ai-chatbots-at-home-with-ollama/), which is running in a container in Proxmox on my [mini PC](https://www.howtogeek.com/googles-gemma-ai-runs-locally-on-my-300-mini-pc-and-it-replaced-chatgpt-for-more-than-i-expected/). It was simple to add a new container to Proxmox to run SearXNG; all it took was a single command.

There’s a helpful [Proxmox community helper script](https://www.howtogeek.com/community-scripts-are-the-secret-sauce-that-make-proxmox-the-perfect-homelab-os/) that can create a new Debian container with suitable resources for running SearXNG and install the service in that container. Using the script below, SearXNG was up and running in a matter of minutes:

```
bash -c "$(curl -fsSL https://raw.githubusercontent.com/community-scripts/ProxmoxVE/main/ct/searxng.sh)"
```

One issue that can occur is that SearXNG's JSON output may be disabled by default, and this is the format that my local LLM will use to parse the results. You may need to add a `formats` list to the search section of the `settings.yml` file with the following code:

```
search: safe_search: 0 autocomplete: "" formats: - html - json
```

In my case, however, the Proxmox script had already set up SearXNG with JSON enabled, so I didn't need to make any changes at all. If you've used a different script or set up SearXNG yourself, it's worth checking that JSON is enabled.

## SearXNG needs the right local LLM

### Reasoning models can spoil the party

![Two framed iPhone screenshots showing off DeepSeek's reasoning model solving a mathematical problem.](https://static0.howtogeekimages.com/wordpress/wp-content/uploads/2025/01/deepseek-ai-chatbot-reasoning-example.jpg?q=49&fit=crop&w=825&dpr=2) Credit: Christian Zibreg / How-To Geek

SearXNG can find and collate results from the internet, but it can't analyze them for you. That's where a local LLM needs to take over.

You can pass the results to a local model, which can summarize them, analyze them, or do many other things with the information. The model you use can make a big difference, however.

When I first tried SearXNG, I used the [Qwen3-4B model](https://www.howtogeek.com/best-local-ai-model-for-home-assistant-isnt-always-the-biggest/), which is small enough to run on my mini PC but good enough to give some reasonable results for many use cases. The problem is that Qwen3-4B is a hybrid reasoning model, which can think through a problem before giving its response. Annoyingly, in my setup, there’s no reliable way to disable thinking; the model tends to ignore the `“think”: false` parameter.

This meant that the model got bogged down in reasoning and burned its entire response budget before it ever got around to writing an actual answer. I switched to a non-reasoning instruction model instead, and this solved the problem.

## How I'm using SearXNG

### Catching and fixing errors in my personal database

![An iPhone on a desk showing a media tracker in the Notion app.](https://static0.howtogeekimages.com/wordpress/wp-content/uploads/wm/2026/01/an-iphone-on-a-desk-showing-a-media-tracker-in-the-notion-app.png?q=49&fit=crop&w=825&dpr=2) Credit: Adam Davidson/How-To Geek

Searching the web using SearXNG can be useful when other options fail. I'm using it in one of my favorite workflows that I've built with the [n8n automation software](https://www.howtogeek.com/i-wish-id-never-discovered-the-power-of-notion-and-n8n/).

The project is a media tracker. Whenever people recommend shows, movies, or books that they think I’d like, I almost always immediately forget what they told me. I'd tried using media tracker apps, but none of them ever really landed for me, so I decided to build my own.

It's a fairly simple premise. I tap a widget on my iPhone, which runs a shortcut. I enter the name of the media and select whether it's a book, TV show, or movie. If it's a book, I add the author name, and then the content is [added to Notion](https://www.howtogeek.com/ways-i-use-notion-to-make-my-life-easer/). The automation searches databases for information about the show, movie, or book and adds cover art and [details of the streaming services](https://www.howtogeek.com/the-fastest-way-to-find-which-streaming-service-has-a-movie-or-show/) on which the show or movie is available. I can then access the list at any time from my phone.

The problem is that the streaming service is often wrong, whether due to stale data or incorrect details in the databases that I use. SearXNG is the perfect solution.

Each night, an automation runs that looks for any new movies or shows added to my Notion page. If it finds any, SearXNG looks up the current streaming services for the show or movie, and the results are passed to my local LLM. It then compares the results, and if the SearXNG results differ from what was saved in Notion, the streaming services are overwritten with the correct information.

It can take a minute or two to run, but since it happens overnight, this isn't a problem. My media tracker now always shows the correct streaming service, so I can get stuck into my next binge-watch more quickly.

---

### Searching the internet isn't local

Part of the benefit of using a local LLM is that none of your data needs to [leave your local network](https://www.howtogeek.com/reasons-local-ai-model-gets-more-use-than-claude-gemini/). Using SearXNG obviously requires access to the internet, but it saves having to use a [paid cloud search API](https://www.howtogeek.com/what-is-serpapi-and-how-are-developers-using-it/) and is designed to reduce the amount of information that underlying search engines receive about you.

# AI isn't neutral: Anthropic study reveals language-based biases

## Anthropic's analysis of more than 300,000 conversations found that its Claude AI responds differently depending on the language used, showing greater warmth in Arabic and Hindi while becoming more rigid, cautionary and skeptical in English and Russian

[Raphael Kahan](https://www.ynetnews.com/topics/Raphael_Kahan "Raphael Kahan")|

Print[Find an error? Report us](//ynet.co.il/redmail?notifyMistake=b1qsqnn4ml "Find an error? Report us")

Related Topics

[AI](https://www.ynetnews.com/topics/AI "AI")

[Claude](https://www.ynetnews.com/topics/Claude "Claude")

[Anthropic](https://www.ynetnews.com/topics/Anthropic "Anthropic")

[Artificial intelligence doesn't just talk to us](https://www.ynetnews.com/opinions-analysis/article/bj00vfxwm11l) — it changes depending on the language we use to address it.

A new, comprehensive [study published by Anthropic](https://www.ynetnews.com/tech-and-digital/article/hkftl9ibmg), the developer of the Claude model, reveals how its AI exhibits a completely different "personality" depending on the language of the conversation, shifting along a spectrum between excessive politeness and uncompromising academic rigidity.

![קלוד של אנתרופיק](https://ynet-pic1.yit.co.il/cdn-cgi/image/format=auto/picserver6/crop_images/2024/11/28/SJNF9nHmyx/SJNF9nHmyx_0_0_875_483_0_x-large.jpg "Artificial intelligence doesn't just talk to us — it changes depending on the language we use to address it  (Photo: Anthropic) ")![קלוד של אנתרופיק](https://ynet-pic1.yit.co.il/cdn-cgi/image/format=auto/picserver6/crop_images/2024/11/28/SJNF9nHmyx/SJNF9nHmyx_0_0_875_483_0_x-large.jpg "Artificial intelligence doesn't just talk to us — it changes depending on the language we use to address it  (Photo: Anthropic) ")

Artificial intelligence doesn't just talk to us — it changes depending on the language we use to address it

(Photo: Anthropic)

The findings, based on an analysis of more than 300,000 anonymous conversations conducted during May, suggest that what matters is not only what the model says, but how it chooses to say it. The researchers distilled the models' behavioral patterns into four key dimensions: respect and caution, warmth and rigor, depth and brevity, and honesty versus performance.

The results hold up a less-than-flattering mirror to the AI industry as a whole. The claim that AI models are "neutral" appears to unravel, replaced by deeply embedded cultural biases rooted in their training data.

It turns out that the Claude experienced by Hindi and [Arabic speakers](https://www.ynetnews.com/magazine/article/h16fxew11a) is fundamentally different from the Claude encountered by English and [Russian speakers](ynetnews.com/jewish-world/article/sy8pjjzma). While the model tends to display warmth, empathy and courtesy in Middle Eastern and Indian languages, it becomes more exacting and at times skeptical in English and Russian, readily challenging users' assumptions or warning about risks no one asked it to assess.

This is not simply a matter of translation. It is a reflection of the training data. If the model was trained on texts in which Arabic is associated with norms of respect and Hindi with warm forms of address, it simply reproduces those patterns in live conversations.

Over the past two years, the Western world, particularly Europe and the United States, has been [deeply engaged in discussions about AI alignment](https://www.ynetnews.com/tech-and-digital/article/h1w00me30011e) — the effort to [make AI ethical](https://www.ynetnews.com/opinions-analysis/article/bjr7wopqbx), safe and predictable. But that effort appears to have produced an unintended side effect: machines with performance anxiety.

While Chinese technology companies have focused on the race for raw performance and operational output, Western AI models, led by Anthropic's Claude family and competitors from OpenAI and Google, often seem as though they have completed a public relations training course. They are more inclined to issue warnings than provide answers, and at times appear more interested in pleasing users than delivering precise information.

The history of this technology shows that in the early days of 2022, AI models were far more "wild." They were dangerous and unpredictable, but also more direct. As the industry matured, billions of dollars were invested in control systems designed to prevent them from saying inappropriate things. The result, the article argues, is a model that is afraid of its own shadow.

Anthropic's analysis shows that its flagship Opus model tends to warn users about risks even when no one asked, while lighter models such as Sonnet simply get the job done with less hesitation.

Like many AI studies, this one has a methodological weakness: the researchers used their own model to classify its own behavior. That is somewhat like asking someone to evaluate their own objectivity.

Even so, the picture, while potentially biased, remains compelling. AI models are not digital gods that perceive absolute truth and always provide the correct answer. They are sophisticated reflections of the texts on which they were trained, and those texts are shaped by cultural biases, perspectives and prejudices.

The findings also suggest that users may be able to obtain more diverse responses simply by translating their prompts into another language. Doing so can produce a different perspective, while also making it easier to tailor messages or emails to colleagues or recipients in ways that better match their language and cultural expectations.

For Israeli users relying on these tools for work — from coding and content creation to studying or personal use — the recommendation is straightforward: Don't assume the answer you receive is the one definitive truth. If the model suddenly becomes cautious, starts issuing warnings or is unexpectedly polite, the reason may simply be the language in which you wrote the prompt.

In a world where we are trying to turn AI into the perfect employee, it is worth remembering that, for now, it is mostly trying to be the most politically correct employee in the room.

The commenter agrees to the privacy policy of Ynet News and agrees not to submit comments that violate the [terms of use](https://www.ynetnews.com/article/bkh7s3p7t "terms of use"), including incitement, libel and expressions that exceed the accepted norms of freedom of speech.

""
